marapendi

  • View page source

Simulation

High-level simulation and parameter-estimation routines.

class marapendi.simulation.estimation.DynamicModel(f, h, x0=[], p=None, u=None, ode_solution_method='BDF')[source]

Bases: object

A class to define, simulate, and analyze non-linear dynamic systems in control engineering notation.

f

State differential equation: x_dot = f(t, x, u, p).

Type:

callable

h

Output equation: y = h(t, x, u, p).

Type:

callable

p

Dictionary of parameters p.

Type:

dict or None

x0

Initial state vector.

Type:

array_like or None

u

Dictionary of inputs u(t).

Type:

dict or None

ode_solution_method

Integration method passed to scipy.integrate.solve_ivp.

Type:

str

__init__(f, h, x0=[], p=None, u=None, ode_solution_method='BDF')[source]

Initialize the dynamic model.

Parameters:

  • f (callable) – State differential equation: x_dot = f(t, x, u, p).

  • h (callable) – Output equation: y = h(t, x, u, p).

  • x0 (array_like, optional) – Initial state vector.

  • p (dict, optional) – Dictionary of parameters p.

  • u (dict, optional) – Dictionary of inputs u(t).

  • ode_solution_method (str, optional) – ODE solver method (default is ‘BDF’).

calculate_local_sensitivity(t, u=None, x0=None, p=None, n_samples=7, measures=None)[source]

Compute local sensitivities by finite differences.

Follows equation 7 in Goshtasbi et al. (2020).

Parameters:

  • t (array_like) – Time vector.

  • u (optional) – Same as above.

  • x0 (optional) – Same as above.

  • p (optional) – Same as above.

  • n_samples (int) – Number of samples per parameter.

Returns:

S – Local sensitivities.

Return type:

ndarray

References

Goshtasbi, A. et al. J. Electrochem. Soc. 167, 044504 (2020).

calculate_local_sensitivity_neighborhood(t, u=None, x0=None, p=None, eps_p=0, measures=None)[source]

Compute local sensitivities by finite differences in the neighborhood of the parameters.

Parameters:

  • t (array_like) – Time vector.

  • u (optional) – Same as above.

  • x0 (optional) – Same as above.

  • p (optional) – Same as above.

  • eps_p (float) – Relative difference for derivative estimation by finite difference.

Returns:

S – Local sensitivities.

Return type:

ndarray

References

Goshtasbi, A. et al. J. Electrochem. Soc. 167, 044504 (2020).

compute_global_sensitivity(t, u=None, x0=None, p=None, n_samples=7, m=8, check_samples=False, y_exp=None, residuals=None, measures=None, rmse_limit=0.3, print_px=False, filename_to_save=None)[source]

Compute global sensitivities using Sobol sampling.

Follows equation 8, 9 and 10 in Goshtasbi et al. (2020).

Parameters:

  • t (optional) – Same as above.

  • u (optional) – Same as above.

  • x0 (optional) – Same as above.

  • p (optional) – Same as above.

  • n_samples (int) – Samples for local sensitivity.

  • m (int) – Generates 2**m Sobol samples.

  • check_samples (bool) – If True, check RMSE against y_exp. Similar to Goshtasbi et al. (2020), but uses L2 norm instead.

  • y_exp (array_like, optional) – Experimental data for RMSE check.

  • rmse_limit (float) – RMSE threshold.

  • print_px (bool) – Print parameter sets.

  • filename_to_save (str) – Filename where to save results. Do not save if None.

Returns:

  • cosPhi_med_ij (ndarray) – Median co-linearity index matrix.

  • norm_s_i (ndarray) – Norm of sensitivities.

  • S_med, S_std (ndarray) – Median and std of sensitivities.

  • S_med_i, S_std_i (ndarray) – Median and std by parameter.

  • S_n (ndarray) – Sensitivity samples.

  • n_valid (int) – Number of valid samples.

References

Goshtasbi, A. et al. J. Electrochem. Soc. 167, 044504 (2020).

estimate(y_exp=None, t=0, u=None, x0=None, p=None, residuals=None, print_iterations=False, popsize=10, workers=1, rtol=0, atol=0, ftol=0, penalty_threshold=0.01, vectorized=False, method='differential_evolution', initial_guess=None, maxiter=120)[source]

Estimate unknown parameters by minimizing the mean of squared errors using differential evolution.

Parameters:

  • y_exp (array_like) – Experimental data.

  • t (array_like) – Time vector.

  • u (optional) – Same as above.

  • x0 (optional) – Same as above.

  • p (optional) – Same as above.

  • print_iterations (bool) – If True, print intermediate results.

  • popsize (control evolution settings.)

  • workers (control evolution settings.)

  • ftol (control evolution settings.)

  • atol (control evolution settings.)

  • penalty_threshold (float) – Threshold to penalize large residuals. See Goshtasi et al. (2020)

Returns:

  • sol (OptimizeResult) – Result from differential_evolution.

  • p_estimated (ndarray) – Estimated parameter values.

References

Goshtasbi, A. et al. J. Electrochem. Soc. 167, 024518 (2020).

get_smallest_hessian_eigenvalues()[source]

Compute the smallest eigenvalues of successive Hessian approximations constructed from the median sensitivity matrix.

This helps to analyze parameter identifiability and numerical conditioning by progressively adding parameters and observing the smallest eigenvalue of the resulting Hessian-like matrix.

Returns:

  • P (ndarray) – Array of parameter indices sorted by importance (from QR decomposition with pivoting).

  • min_eigvals (list of float) – List of smallest eigenvalues for each incremental Hessian matrix, starting from 1 parameter up to all parameters.

  • num_parameters (int) – Total number of parameters considered.

Notes

  • Uses QR decomposition with column pivoting to identify influential parameters.

  • The Hessian approximation is built as H = S_selected x S_selected.T

where S_selected contains rows of the median sensitivity matrix. - Small eigenvalues close to zero indicate collinearity or poor identifiability.

References

Goshtasbi, A. et al. J. Electrochem. Soc. 167, 044504 (2020). Lund, B. F. & Foss, B. A. Automatica (Oxf.) 44, 278–281 (2008).

load_global_sensitivity_results(filename)[source]

Load global sensitivity results from file.

Parameters:

filname (str) – Filename where results are stored.

p_to_theta(unknown_p_values)[source]
plot(i=0, fig=None, ax=None)[source]

Plot the i-th state trajectory.

Parameters:

  • i (int) – Index of state to plot.

  • fig (optional) – Matplotlib figure and axis.

  • ax (optional) – Matplotlib figure and axis.

Returns:

fig, ax

Return type:

Matplotlib objects.

plot_colinearity_map(fig=None, ax=None, cmap='viridis', xlabel_angle=45, figsize=(4, 3), write_text=True)[source]

Plot the median co-linearity indexes matrix.

Parameters:

  • fig (optional) – Matplotlib figure and axis.

  • ax (optional) – Matplotlib figure and axis.

  • cmap (str) – Colormap name.

  • xlabel_angle (float) – Rotation angle.

  • figsize (tuple) – Figure size.

  • write_text (bool) – Annotate cells.

Returns:

fig, ax

Return type:

Matplotlib objects.

plot_global_sensitivity(fig=None, ax=None, cmap='viridis', color='C0', xlabel_angle=45, xlabel_ha='center', figsize=(4, 3), parameter_order=[])[source]

Plot global sensitivity metrics.

Parameters:

  • fig (optional) – Matplotlib figure and axis.

  • ax (optional) – Matplotlib figure and axis.

  • cmap (str) – Colormap name.

  • color (str) – Line color.

  • xlabel_angle (float) – Rotation angle of labels.

  • figsize (tuple) – Figure size.

Returns:

fig, ax

Return type:

Matplotlib objects.

plot_parameter_ranking()[source]

Plot the ranking of parameters based on smallest eigenvalues of the Hessian matrix.

Parameters:

  • filename (str) – (Currently unused in function, could be for saving figure).

  • model (object) – Model containing the list of unknown parameters.

Returns:

  • P (ndarray) – Permutation indices indicating parameter ranking.

  • num_parameters (int) – Total number of parameters.

  • fig, ax, ax2 (Matplotlib objects) – Figure and axes for further customization or saving.

residuals(y_exp, t, u=None, x0=None, p=None)[source]

Compute residuals between experimental data and model predictions.

Parameters:

  • y_exp (array_like) – Experimental output data.

  • t (array_like) – Time vector.

  • u (dict, optional) – Inputs.

  • x0 (array_like, optional) – Initial state.

  • p (dict, optional) – Parameters.

Returns:

residuals – Differences y_exp - y_model.

Return type:

ndarray

set_conditions(u)[source]

Set or update the dictionary of input functions u(t).

Parameters:

u (dict) – Dictionary of inputs.

set_initial_conditions(x0)[source]

Set or update initial conditions.

Parameters:

x0 (array_like) – Initial state vector.

set_params(p)[source]

Set or update the dictionary of model parameters.

Parameters:

p (dict) – Dictionary of parameters.

set_unknown_params(p_list)[source]

Define the parameters to be estimated, their bounds and types.

Parameters:

p_list (list of tuples) – Each tuple is (name, (min, max), is_linear, label).

solve(time, u=None, x0=[], parameters_dict=None, rtol=0.0001, atol=1e-05, vectorized=False, sparsity=None)[source]

Solve the ODE system over the given time vector.

Parameters:

  • time (array_like) – Time points for evaluation.

  • u (dict, optional) – Input dictionary (overrides self.u).

  • x0 (array_like, optional) – Initial conditions (overrides self.x0).

  • parameters_dict (dict, optional) – Parameters (overrides self.p).

Returns:

  • t (ndarray) – Time vector.

  • x (ndarray) – State trajectories.

  • y (ndarray) – Output trajectories.

theta_to_p(theta_k)[source]

Convert normalized parameters to physical parameters.

Follows equations 5 and 6 in Goshtasbi et al. (2020).

Parameters:

theta_k (array_like) – Normalized parameters in [0,1].

Returns:

p_i_k – Physical parameters.

Return type:

ndarray

References

Goshtasbi, A. et al. J. Electrochem. Soc. 167, 044504 (2020).

class marapendi.simulation.estimation.SteadyStateModel(h, p=None)[source]

Bases: DynamicModel

A sub-class for steady-state models, where the output is directly computed from the parameters without integrating any dynamic equations.

Parameters:

  • h (callable) – Measurement function. It should compute the output y given the parameters p.

  • p (dict, optional) – Dictionary of parameters.

h

The measurement function used to compute the outputs.

Type:

callable

p

Dictionary of parameters.

Type:

dict

__init__(h, p=None)[source]

Initialize the steady-state parameter estimation model.

Parameters:

  • h (callable) – Measurement function y = h(p).

  • p (dict, optional) – Dictionary of parameters.

solve(time, u=None, x0=None, parameters_dict=None)[source]

Compute the model output at steady state.

Since this is a steady-state model, it does not integrate any ODEs. It simply computes the output y directly from the parameters.

Parameters:

  • time (array-like) – Time vector (not used here but kept for API compatibility).

  • u (any, optional) – Input (not used in this steady-state model).

  • x0 (any, optional) – Initial conditions (not used).

  • parameters_dict (dict, optional) – Dictionary of parameters. If None, uses self.p.

Returns:

  • t (int) – Placeholder (0), since time is not evolved in steady state.

  • x (int) – Placeholder (0), since there are no state trajectories.

  • y (array-like) – Output computed by the measurement function h.
